metadata.summary: Coordinated introduction of a product to market. Plan 6-8 weeks ahead using T-8-to-T+2 timeline with asset checklist, channel matrix, success metrics, and rollback narrative.

content.title: Product Launch
content.summary: A product launch is the coordinated introduction of a product to its target market. Plan 6-8 weeks ahead using a T-8-to-T+2 timeline. Required for any public or beta launch: a launch DRI, an asset checklist (landing page, announcement email, demo/screenshots, social posts, documentation), a channel-per-timing matrix, success metrics with targets set before launch day, and a rollback narrative written before launch. Launch Tuesday or Wednesday, mid-month, avoiding major industry events.

why.p: Launches without a structured playbook either underwhelm (ship quietly, no momentum) or collapse into last-minute chaos. The T-8 timeline forces asset creation and audience building well before launch day; the asset checklist prevents the single most common failure (missing materials discovered at T-0). A rollback narrative written in advance removes decision paralysis on launch day — if you never publish it, no harm done.

list.item: Public release with a coordinated marketing moment (Product Hunt, press, partner amplification).
Major version bump where existing users need migration messaging and new acquisition push.
Beta-to-GA transition where pricing, positioning, or audience changes.
Geographic or segment expansion of an existing product into a new market.
Bug-fix releases or quiet shipping behind a feature flag — use a release-notes template, not a launch playbook.
Solo founder shipping iteratively to fewer than 100 users — the playbook overhead exceeds the value; ship to a Discord and write a tweet.
Internal tools — coordinate with stakeholders, but the GTM machinery is unnecessary.
Markets where a loud launch backfires (regulated industries, B2B enterprise where field sales owns timing).

section.name: planning-and-timeline
section.p: A successful launch begins 6-8 weeks before the public announcement. The T-8-to-T+2 timeline forces asset creation and audience building well before launch day; the asset checklist prevents the single most common failure (missing materials discovered at T-0). The 8-week timeline assumes a marketing function exists; for a solo PM the same checklist takes 12-16 weeks of part-time work.

rule.statement: Launches MUST follow a T-8-to-T+2 timeline with a locked launch date 2 weeks out, treated as immovable. The timeline MUST include: positioning and messaging definition (T-8 to T-6), asset creation (T-6 to T-2), audience pre-build and partner coordination (T-6 to T-1), launch-day checklist with sequence steps (T-0), and post-launch monitoring (T+1 to T+7).
rule.rationale: Sliding the launch date erodes team trust and breaks partner commitments. A fixed timeline forces discipline in prioritization and prevents last-minute feature creep.

rule.statement: Launch dates MUST be Tuesday or Wednesday, mid-month, and MUST avoid US holidays and major industry events (re:Invent, Google I/O, WWDC weeks).
rule.rationale: Tuesday/Wednesday captures the full weekday attention cycle without weekend bleed-through. Mid-month avoids the monthly-billing noise. Industry event weeks fragment media attention.

example.title: T-8 timeline with locked date
example.code: T-8 (June 17): Positioning workshop + audience definition T-6 (June 24): Asset creation begins (landing page, demo video, email template) T-4 (July 1): Partner outreach to 5 warm hunters / press contacts T-2 (July 8): Final asset review + rollback narrative draft T-1 (July 14): Pre-launch checklist + channel matrix finalized T-0 (July 15, Tuesday, 12:01 AM PT): Launch execution T+1 (July 16): Testimonial capture window opens T+7 (July 22): Postmortem scheduled
example.note: Date locked July 15, Tuesday. No slip. All assets staged by T-2. Rollback narrative written but never published.

example.title: Floating launch date with no playbook
example.code: Let's ship when it feels ready. The landing page can wait until T-3. We'll figure out press outreach next week.
example.note: No locked date. No timeline. Missing materials discovered at T-0. Launch happens mid-afternoon Friday without coordination. Underwhelms.

antipattern.description: Sliding the launch date because "we need more time to polish" or "one more feature."
antipattern.reason: Each slip breaks partner commitments (hunters, press, amplification partners) and erodes team urgency. The polish is rarely meaningful; the reputational cost is real.

section.name: asset-checklist
section.p: The asset checklist prevents the single most common launch failure: discovering critical materials are missing at T-0. Every asset has a defined owner and "definition of done" before launch day.

rule.statement: Each launch MUST include a written asset checklist with rows for: landing page (with sign-up CTA), announcement email (with testimonials or social proof), product demo or screenshot walkthrough, Twitter/X thread (5-8 tweets), LinkedIn post, Product Hunt tagline and description (if applicable), press release or media kit, internal launch guide for the team, and API/technical documentation if applicable. Each row MUST name an owner and a "definition of done" (e.g., "landing page: reviewed by 2 external users, zero 404s, <3 second load time").
rule.rationale: Ownership eliminates blame-shifting. Explicit done criteria prevent scope creep and typo oversights. Missing a single asset (landing page screenshot, email subject line) tanks momentum.

example.title: Filled asset checklist
example.code: | Asset | Owner | Status | Definition of Done | |-------|-------|--------|-------------------| | Landing page | Alice | Done | CTA visible above fold, load less than 3s, 0 404s, 2 external review | | Demo video | Bob | In progress | 90 sec, captions, hosted on YouTube, shareable link | | Tweet thread | Carol | ✅ Done | 5 tweets, hook+benefit+social-proof+CTA, no emojis, checked dates/links | | Email announcement | Alice | Pending | Subject line <50 chars, testimonial, unsubscribe, idempotent resend | | Product Hunt | Dana | ✅ Done | Tagline <60 chars, description, logo (500x500), press quote, PH url verified |
example.note: Ownership is explicit. Done criteria are measurable. Nothing vague. Reviewer sign-off captured in the status.

antipattern.description: Creating a vague checklist like "marketing assets" or "everything ready" without owner names or done criteria.
antipattern.reason: Vague checklists create diffusion of responsibility. On launch day, critical pieces are missing because no one thought they owned them.

section.name: channel-matrix-and-timing
section.p: Different channels have different optimal timing windows. The channel matrix defines which asset goes to which channel and when, from T-7 (warm-up phase) through T+7 (follow-up phase).

rule.statement: Launches MUST include a channel-per-timing matrix with rows for: Product Hunt (T-0 12:01 AM PT), Twitter/X (T-0 morning, then Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4), LinkedIn (T-0 mid-day), email list (T-0 or T+1), press contacts (T-7 warm-up, T-0 embargo lift), and partner amplification (T+1 to T+3). Each row MUST specify: asset, timing, format (thread, single post, segment), CTA variant, and monitoring signal (mentions, retweets, signup spike).
rule.rationale: Channel timing maximizes awareness. Product Hunt at T-0 00:01 captures early momentum; Twitter morning drives daylong conversation; LinkedIn reaches professionals mid-day; email converts warm leads. Staggered partner posts (T+1 to T+3) sustain long-tail traffic.

rule.statement: Twitter/X MUST receive at least one post per day from T-0 to T+3 (the "3-day tail"). Posting only once at T-0 leaves the launch to die mid-afternoon.
rule.rationale: Twitter's feed algorithm decays posts rapidly. Sustained posting (morning, mid-day, evening) keeps the launch visible. Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4 day posts remind followers and catch different timezones.

example.title: Channel matrix
example.code: | Channel | Timing | Asset | Format | CTA | Monitor | |---------|--------|-------|--------|-----|---------| | Product Hunt | T-0 00:01 AM PT | PH tagline + description | 1 submission | Sign up | PH ranking #1-10 | | Twitter | T-0 09:00 AM | Tweet thread (5 tweets) | Thread | "Try it" link | Retweets, replies | | Twitter | T-0 12:00 PM | Screenshot + outcome | Single post | "Join 500 users" | Likes, signups | | LinkedIn | T-0 03:00 PM | CEO post + founding story | Single post | "Read the story" | Comments, inbound | | Email list | T-0 06:00 PM | Announcement + urgency | Newsletter | "Claim early access" | Open rate, CTR | | Press | T-7 onward | Media kit PDF + 3-line summary | Email | (embargo) | Pickup by T+1 |
example.note: Timing is granular. Each asset has a unique format and CTA. Monitoring metrics are explicit.

section.name: success-metrics-and-targets
section.p: Success metrics and targets MUST be written before launch day. Defining them after launch is self-serving. Include awareness (impressions, reach), acquisition (signups, users), engagement (DAU, retention at T+7), and satisfaction (NPS, testimonials).

rule.statement: Each launch MUST define 3-5 success metrics before launch day, with targets set and realistic anchors derived from comparable launches. Metrics MUST include: awareness (impressions, reach, mentions), acquisition (signups, free-trial conversions), engagement (DAU, feature adoption at T+7), and long-term retention (retention at T+30). Targets MUST NOT be aspirational (100K signups for a solo founder) — they MUST be grounded in comparable product launches in the same category.
rule.rationale: Pre-set targets prevent post-hoc celebration of mediocre numbers. Historical anchors (e.g., "similar SaaS products see 50-200 signups in week 1") ground expectations. Aspirational targets train teams to ignore reality and repeat failures.

example.title: Grounded success metrics
example.code: Launch target: 150 signups in week 1 (comparable: 3 similar tools averaged 120-180) Twitter reach: 50K impressions (comparable PH + warm network: historical 40-70K) Press coverage: 3 tech blogs, 1 newsletter mention (realistic for unseeded launch) Retention: 30% of week-1 signups return in week 2 (typical for first-time SaaS) NPS from testimonials: 8+ (capture in first 48 hours when goodwill is high)
example.note: Each target references a comparable baseline. No wild aspirations. Metrics are owned and tracked.

antipattern.description: LLMs or overeager teams announcing "we expect 100K signups, top 3 on Product Hunt" without grounding in comparable launches.
antipattern.reason: Unrealistic targets train the team to ignore actual performance and repeat failures. The public announcement creates reputational pressure to spin results.

section.name: rollback-narrative
section.p: A rollback narrative is a 200-word post explaining why the launch was paused or rolled back. Write it before launch. If you never publish it, no harm done. If things go sideways (critical bug, unexpected backlash, data breach), the narrative is ready and buys time for the team to respond thoughtfully instead of panicking in public.

rule.statement: Every launch MUST include a pre-written rollback narrative (150-250 words) covering: what happened, why it happened, what the team is doing now, and when the launch resumes. The narrative MUST NOT appear on social or the website — it is a contingency held by the launch DRI. If a critical issue arises (data breach, massive bug, supply-chain failure), the DRI publishes it within 4 hours of identifying the issue, then takes the launch offline.
rule.rationale: A prepared statement prevents panic tweets and preserves customer trust. Without it, teams scramble to craft a response mid-crisis and often say something wrong. Having it written in advance (when calm) means the response is thoughtful and buys time for the real fix.

example.title: Rollback narrative
example.code: We've paused the launch to investigate a data consistency issue discovered in our QA environment. No customer data is affected. Our team is implementing a fix and adding additional test coverage. We expect to resume the launch on [date] and will post an update here. Thank you for your patience. In the meantime, [join the waitlist / read the roadmap / subscribe to updates].
example.note: 200 words exactly. Transparent. Provides a timeline and alternative action. Not defensive.

section.name: launch-day-execution
section.p: Launch day is about sequencing, not improvisation. The launch-day checklist defines what to do at each time window, who is responsible, and what signals indicate something is going wrong.

rule.statement: The launch-day checklist MUST include: pre-launch checks (T-30 min: login to all systems, test asset links, verify analytics dashboards), go-live steps (T-0: deploy backend feature flag, publish landing page, submit to Product Hunt, email list blast), announcement sequence (T+0-6 hours: stagger social posts every 2 hours), monitoring (T+0-24 hours: Slack webhook posting signup and error metrics every 15 min), and EOD debrief (T+1: first pass on what worked, what didn't). One person is the launch DRI with kill-switch authority.
rule.rationale: Sequencing prevents accidentally publishing before the landing page is live or forgetting to turn on analytics. A single DRI prevents conflicting decisions during the chaos. Staggered posts keep momentum. Monitoring alerts the team to problems (error spike, checkout bug) in real time.

rule.statement: NO automatic social posts on launch day. All Twitter, LinkedIn, and email sends MUST pass through a human approval gate within 60 minutes of the scheduled time. Auto-publishing to social on launch day is a reputational landmine — typos, broken links, and offensive language go live before anyone catches them.
rule.rationale: Humans catch visual issues (broken images, wrong screenshot), typos, and tone problems that LLMs miss. The 60-minute window is tight enough to maintain momentum but gives a human time to review and approve.

example.title: Launch-day checklist
example.code:
**T-30 min:** - [ ] Login to Product Hunt, verify submission draft - [ ] Test landing page: navigation, sign-up form, payment (if applicable) - [ ] Open analytics dashboard (Plausible, Umami) in 4 browser tabs - [ ] Verify Slack webhook is posting metrics - [ ] Notify team: 30 min until go-live
**T-0 (12:01 AM PT):** - [ ] Feature flag enabled (backend) - [ ] Landing page published (DNS live) - [ ] Product Hunt: publish submission - [ ] Email: send announcement to list
**T+2 hours (02:00 AM PT):** - [ ] Post first Twitter thread (hook + demo + CTA) - [ ] Monitor: 1st signup spike visible?
**T+4 hours:** - [ ] LinkedIn post (CEO story)
**T+6 hours:** - [ ] Pause, check metrics (signups, errors). Any red flags? - [ ] Eat breakfast. You've earned it.
**T+24 hours:** - [ ] Debrief: what worked, what flopped, lessons for next time
example.note: Sequence is explicit. Human approval gates are clear. Kill-switch holder is named (DRI). Monitoring is continuous.

antipattern.description: Automating all social posts on launch day, then watching a typo or broken link go live to 50K followers.
antipattern.reason: Bots miss visual issues and tone problems. A human review gate (60 min) catches these before publish.

section.name: post-launch-and-retrospective
section.p: The launch doesn't end at T+1. Capture testimonials in the 48 hours after launch (when goodwill is highest), run a 30-minute postmortem within 7 days, and keep momentum with follow-up posts through T+7.

rule.statement: Within 48 hours of launch, the team MUST capture 5-10 customer testimonials (one-liner + name + company/role). Goodwill is highest when the product is fresh and novel; after 2 weeks, enthusiasm fades. These quotes go into future marketing (landing page, case studies, social proof).
rule.rationale: Testimonials collected immediately are authentic and effusive. Testimonials requested weeks later are polished but generic and hard to get. Ask within 48 hours: "What was your biggest aha moment with [product]?"

rule.statement: A 30-minute postmortem MUST run within 7 days of launch. Attendance: launch DRI, marketing, product, and engineering leads. Agenda: (1) What was the target vs. what we hit? (2) What surprised us (good or bad)? (3) What's one thing we'd do differently? Document and share with the team. If you wait more than a week, lessons evaporate.
rule.rationale: Lessons fade quickly. A structured postmortem within a week captures learnings while memory is fresh. Waiting a month means the insights are lost and the team repeats the same mistakes on the next launch.

antipattern.description: Skipping the postmortem because "the launch is over, on to the next thing."
antipattern.reason: Without reflection, the team repeats the same mistakes on every launch. Each launch becomes chaos instead of a repeatable playbook that gets incrementally better.

section.name: agentic-workflows
section.p: AI agents can accelerate launch planning and execution. Recommended subagents: a launch-planner agent that drafts the timeline and checklist, a copy-variant agent that generates channel-specific copy, a monitor agent that watches launch-day metrics, and a retrospective agent (human-in-loop) that analyzes performance post-launch.

rule.statement: LLM copy agents MUST NOT be allowed to use: revolutionary, game-changing, leverage, paradigm, seamless, robust, cutting-edge, disruptive, innovative. These words are red flags for inflation. Agents drafting marketing copy MUST operate under a banned-words list and a requirement for 2 human review rounds minimum.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to hype. A banned-words list and multi-round human review forces specificity. Actual benefits (speed, cost savings, simplicity) sell better than vague superlatives.

rule.statement: DO NOT let agents draft the post-launch retrospective from metrics alone. Force them to interview 3 customers first and incorporate qualitative feedback. Metrics-only retrospectives turn self-congratulatory and miss real user pain points.
rule.rationale: Numbers can be spun. Hearing directly from users ("the onboarding was confusing" or "the pricing modal scared me away") reveals the actual story behind the metrics.

example.title: Agentic launch workflow
example.code:
Step 1: Launch-planner agent (Sonnet) Input: product name, audience, launch date, success metrics Output: T-8 to T+2 timeline, asset checklist, channel matrix
Step 2: Copy-variant agent (Sonnet) Input: positioning brief, channel list, audience persona Output: 5-8 Twitter posts, LinkedIn post, PH tagline, email subject lines (human reviews all)
Step 3: Checklist agent Input: timeline + asset checklist Output: GitHub Issues (dated, owner, definition of done)
Step 4: Monitor agent (launch day, real-time) Input: Plausible API, Twitter API, signup form webhook Output: Slack messages every 15 min with signup count, visitor count, error rate (human watches for anomalies)
Step 5: Retrospective agent (T+8, human-in-loop) Input: metrics, 3 customer interviews, team notes Output: Structured report (what worked, what flopped, one lesson for next time)
example.note: Agents handle data collection and synthesis. Humans own approval, final messaging, and decision-making.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Tooling landscape for launch planning and execution. Most tools have APIs; agent-friendly ones are called out.

rule.statement: The launch tech stack MUST include: one analytics tool (Plausible, Fathom, or Umami) for privacy-respecting launch-day metrics, one email service (Resend, Beehiiv, or ConvertKit) for announcement blasts, one link shortener or utm builder for tracking (Short.io, Bitly), and one monitoring tool (Sentry or Highlight.io) to catch errors on launch day.
rule.rationale: Analytics is non-negotiable (you must watch traffic). Email is non-negotiable (you must contact the list). UTM tracking is non-negotiable (you must attribute signups to channels). Error monitoring is non-negotiable (you must catch bugs before customers report them). All of these have APIs, so agents can monitor and report in real time.

example.title: Launch day monitoring setup
example.code:
# Plausible stats: visitors, signups, referrer traffic curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $PLAUSIBLE_TOKEN" \ "https://plausible.io/api/v1/stats/aggregate?site_id=$SITE&period=day&metrics=visitors,events,bounce_rate" | jq .
# Product Hunt API: current ranking curl -s "https://api.producthunt.com/v2/graphql" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $PH_TOKEN" \ -d '{ "query": "{ post(id: \"product-slug\") { rank rating votesCount } }" }' | jq .
# Sentry: error rate on launch day curl -s "https://sentry.io/api/0/projects/org/project/stats/?resolution=1h&stat=errors" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $SENTRY_TOKEN" | jq .
example.note: APIs return JSON. Agent can parse and send alerts to Slack every 15 minutes.

template.purpose: Full launch plan template: overview, audience, timeline table, asset checklist, channel matrix, risk mitigation.
Launch-day runbook: pre-launch checks, go-live steps, announcement sequence, monitoring, EOD debrief.
Bash monitor script: polls signup and visitor metrics every 5 minutes, posts to Slack webhook.
